results.possibleIdentity: David McFarlane was a Canadian advertising designer active in Toronto during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was employed by Stewart & Morrison Limited, which was at the time Canada's largest and most prestigious graphic design firm, known for creating iconic identities such as those for Air Canada, the Montreal Expos, and the Toronto-Dominion Bank. McFarlane's technical focus was in advertising design. He was associated with the Toronto chapter of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded to foster collaboration between artists and the technical community (engineers and scientists). His involvement with E.A.T. reflects the era's movement towards interdisciplinary collaboration between commercial design, technology, and the fine arts.
